findings.message: `spec.json` does not cover the full planned track. `metadata.json`/`plan.md` add `T003` (`track-signals` wrapper + `track:plan` procedure update) and `T004` (CI verification), but `scope.in_scope` only lists `T001`/`T002`, and the CI acceptance criterion is tagged `T003` instead of `T004`. As written, the approved spec can be satisfied without ever reviewing the wrapper/procedure change, and the real CI task is left untraced.

`cargo make ci` is incorrectly tagged as `T003`, but `T003` in `metadata.json` is the track-signals / `track:plan` update task and `T004` is the CI check. `T003` is also missing from `in_scope`, so the spec leaves the planned wrapper/command work under-scoped and misassigns the CI acceptance criterion.

`spec.json` never turns task `T003` into an acceptance criterion. The plan and metadata make the `track-signals` wrapper plus the `track:plan` step update part of the intended deliverable, but the acceptance section only covers `T001`, `T002`, and CI. As written, this track could be accepted without implementing the entire `T003` scope.

The `T002` requirement/criterion cites `knowledge/conventions/source-attribution.md` as its provenance, but that document only describes how source tags should be recorded and explicitly warns against fabricating references; it does not justify changing the `full-cycle` semantics in `SKILL.md`. This leaves the item with unsupported provenance, which breaks the traceability this spec is supposed to provide.

`verification.md` already marks the first two scope items as verified even though every manual verification step is still unchecked and the result section says `_ćśŞĺ®źć–˝_`. That gives a false positive audit trail for pre-implementation work and can mislead downstream reviewers about what has actually been validated.

`spec.json` still has no acceptance criterion for T003 (`cargo make track-signals` wrapper plus the `track:plan` spec-approve/track-signals step update). That means the track can satisfy all listed acceptance criteria without ever checking one of its scoped deliverables, so this workflow change could be omitted and still look complete.

`verification.md` marks the `full-cycle.md` rewrite and `SKILL.md` update as already verified, but this track is still at the planning-artifact stage and the same file says verification is `_ćśŞĺ®źć–˝_`. Those false-positive `[x]` entries can mislead reviewers into treating T001/T002 as completed without any actual verification evidence.

`T002`'s provenance is still unsupported: both the scope item and matching acceptance criterion cite `knowledge/conventions/source-attribution.md`, but that document only defines how sources must be recorded and explicitly says not to fabricate references. It does not justify changing the `full-cycle` semantics in `SKILL.md`, so this requirement is not traceable to an actual design/input source.

`T001` is still under-specified. `metadata.json` says the new `/track:full-cycle` flow must also confirm commit messages by default, support `--auto` message generation, and stop with a status report on failure, but `spec.json`/`verification.md` never make any of those behaviors part of scope or acceptance. As written, this track can be approved after only changing the loop shape while dropping the new commit UX and fail-fast semantics.

The approved spec defines the per-task cycle as `implement -> review -> commit` and leaves `cargo make ci` as a separate `T004` check. That conflicts with `track/workflow.md`, where the required task workflow is `review -> cargo make ci -> commit`. In other words, this plan currently allows `/track:full-cycle` to create task commits before any CI gate has passed.

`T001` is still under-specified here. `metadata.json` section `S1` requires not just the per-task implementâ†’reviewâ†’commit loop, but also default commit-message confirmation, `--auto` commit-message generation, and fail-fast status reporting. `spec.json` only accepts the loop shape, and `verification.md` only checks that loop structure, so this track can be marked complete while dropping the new commit UX and failure semantics promised by the plan.

This approved spec carries a stale `content_hash`. Recomputing it from the current hashable fields yields `sha256:3c65471ed1e2b16fb66fc464f9527480b1c4bf785d7709ad10c0ebf12f72b475`, not the stored `sha256:21e27c25cafa5ecab6d37c53ebf8c02bec6f44f78b2a756402103b437428e5d4`. Because the approval gate auto-demotes on hash mismatch, any consumer decoding this file will treat it as draft even though `status` still says `approved`.

`spec.json` still carries a stale approval hash. Recomputing the hash from the current hashable fields yields `sha256:c205a22b042b39a71990f3e055d18781a56cd3e3f6671d1119b9236454c8445d`, not the stored `sha256:f1182363b4a31db75a859a37c54d01fce1223287b4a23e290b844f60db2d835f`. Because spec decoding auto-demotes approved documents on hash mismatch, consumers will treat this spec as unapproved even though `status` remains `approved`.

`spec.json` still defines the new `/track:full-cycle` semantics as a per-task `implement â†’ review â†’ commit` loop while leaving `cargo make ci` as a separate `T004` check. That contradicts the repository workflow in `track/workflow.md`, which requires `review â†’ cargo make ci â†’ commit` for each task. As written, this plan would allow task commits before the mandatory CI gate has passed, so the approved behavior can violate the project's own quality-gate policy.

`spec.json` uses `conversation:*` source strings (for example `conversation:user-request-2026-04-08`), but the source-attribution convention only defines `feedback â€” ...`, `discussion`, `convention â€” ...`, `inference â€” ...`, or real document references. `sotp track signals` will classify these conversation tags as document evidence and score them Blue, overstating confidence for requirements that actually came from discussion/feedback.

The spec says `/track:full-cycle` must stop being "transitional compatibility only", but T001's metadata plan only tracks the per-task loop/commit behavior. Because that requirement is not traced into the task plan (and therefore not into the generated verification checklist), this track can be marked complete while leaving the existing transitional-only wording in `.claude/commands/track/full-cycle.md` unchanged.

`metadata.json` was edited in this change, but `updated_at` is still the original `2026-04-10T12:00:00+09:00` even though the track artifacts were updated later (`spec.json` was re-approved at `2026-04-10T12:43:22Z`). Latest-track resolution and `verify-latest-track` both key off `metadata.json.updated_at`, so leaving it stale can cause this track to be skipped or misordered during completeness checks.

T001 now explicitly includes removing the `transitional compatibility` positioning and treating `/track:full-cycle` as the formal command, but the T001 acceptance criterion still only checks the task-loop/CI/commit behavior. Because `verification.md` mirrors that criterion and has no step for the wording/status change, the new requirement can be missed while the track is marked verified.

`metadata.json`/`plan.md` now make T001 responsible for removing the `transitional compatibility` positioning and treating `/track:full-cycle` as the formal command, but the T001 acceptance criterion here still only checks the loop/CI/commit behavior. Because `verification.md` mirrors this criterion and never asks reviewers to confirm the wording/status change, the track can be marked complete while leaving the old transitional-only wording in `.claude/commands/track/full-cycle.md` untouched.

`spec.json` still lets T001 pass after merely inserting a review step before CI/commit, because the acceptance criterion no longer requires that review to reach `zero_findings`. `metadata.json` S1 still defines the loop as `review (zero_findings) -> cargo make ci -> commit`, so the current criterion leaves a real loophole where `/track:full-cycle` could proceed past a failing review and still satisfy the spec.

`verification.md` no longer verifies the `zero_findings` gate for T001. `metadata.json` section `S1` and `spec.json` both require `implement â†’ review (zero_findings) â†’ cargo make ci â†’ commit`, but the checklist here only asks reviewers to confirm a generic `implement â†’ review â†’ cargo make ci â†’ commit` loop. That means `/track:full-cycle` could still pass this verification plan even if it proceeds to CI/commit after a review that reported findings.

`metadata.json.updated_at` is still older than the latest change in this track: it was set to `2026-04-10T21:50:00+09:00`, but `spec.json` was re-approved later at `2026-04-10T12:53:41Z` (`2026-04-10T21:53:41+09:00`). Latest-track resolution and completeness checks key off `metadata.json.updated_at`, so leaving it behind the actual latest artifact update can misorder this track or make downstream checks read stale track state.

The updated `/track:plan` next-step template still tells users to run only `cargo make spec-approve <track-dir>` before starting implementation. T003 and the new `track-signals` wrapper make `spec-approve -> track-signals` the intended post-approval flow, so this changed guidance is still incomplete and will leave users skipping signal evaluation/persistence.

The updated `/track:plan` next-step templates still instruct users to run only `cargo make spec-approve <track-dir>` after approval. T003 adds `track-signals` as part of the required post-approval flow, and this same skill already documents `bin/sotp track signals <track-id>` elsewhere, so users following these templates will skip signal evaluation and leave the spec/views stale.

`metadata.json` still encodes T003's plan text with the old `spec-approve -> track-signals` order, while the task description, spec, verification checklist, and updated SKILL now require `track-signals -> spec-approve`. Because `metadata.json` is the SSoT for the rendered plan, this stale section will keep regenerating contradictory instructions and leaves the track artifacts out of sync about the canonical approval flow.

`Makefile.toml` adds a new public `cargo make track-signals` wrapper, but the wrapper regression suite is not updated to cover it. That leaves the new dispatch/arg-forwarding path unverified, so a typo or later refactor of `bin/sotp track signals "$@"` can break the command without CI catching it.

The commit phase is incomplete: `cargo make track-commit-message` needs `tmp/track-commit/commit-message.txt`, and `cargo make track-note` needs `tmp/track-commit/note.md`, but Step 1e never tells the command to write either scratch file. As written, the autonomous loop cannot reliably commit and may fail or reuse stale note/message content.

This resolution order never handles `plan/<id>` checkouts. If `/track:full-cycle` is run from a planning-only branch, it will fall through to some other active track instead of stopping with `/track:activate`, so the command can operate on the wrong track.

The loop only processes tasks with `status == "todo"`, but failure handling leaves the failing task `in_progress` and tells the user to rerun `/track:full-cycle`. On rerun, that task will be skipped, so the command cannot resume the blocked task it just stopped on.

Approving `spec.json` before `track-signals` mutates it makes the recorded `content_hash` stale as soon as signals are written. Since approval validity depends on the current file contents, this workflow should evaluate signals first or re-run approval after the signals update.

The failure-handling block does not cover the `cargo make track-transition <track_dir> <task_id> done --commit-hash <hash>` step. If the done transition fails after the task has already been committed, the loop has no instruction to stop or recover, so the task can stay `in_progress` while the implementation proceeds as if it were finished.

The post-loop `verification.md` update and `cargo make track-sync-views` run are never followed by a staging or commit step. A successful full-cycle therefore leaves the branch dirty, and the final verification / regenerated view changes will be missing from the PR.

`/track:full-cycle` still falls back to the latest active track when you are not on a `track/<id>` branch, but the new loop now performs guarded commits. That means invoking it from `main` or another non-track branch can commit against the wrong track and skip `track-commit-message`'s branch/hash bookkeeping, because that wrapper only enforces those checks on `track/<id>` branches. Require the implementation branch before entering the commit loop or switch to it first.

The failure handler treats a `cargo make track-transition ... done` error the same as a pre-commit failure and tells the operator to rerun `/track:full-cycle`. If the commit already succeeded, that rerun will re-enter implement/review/CI for the same task and can create a duplicate commit instead of just retrying the missing `done` transition.

The `track:plan` handoff inverts the requested `spec-approve -> track-signals` sequence: the success path says to run `track-signals` before `spec-approve`, and the fallback path approves first and only then retries signals. That leaves the canonical planning guide with an approval step that can still be followed by a later `spec.json` mutation without a matching re-approval pass.

The `/track:plan` handoff is still inverted relative to the track definition: this doc runs `track-signals` before approval, even though the task spec says the canonical sequence should be `spec-approve â†’ track-signals`. That leaves the planner instruction out of sync with the accepted workflow, and the failure branch can approve a spec before the signal step is actually resolved.

The `/track:plan` handoff is still inverted relative to the requested canonical sequence: the normal path runs `track-signals` before `spec-approve`, and the fallback path approves first and only then retries signals. That leaves the planning guide out of sync with the intended `spec-approve -> track-signals` flow.

Post-commit failures are not recoverable in this loop. `cargo make track-commit-message` can already have created the commit and still return a non-zero post-commit status, and `cargo make track-note` can fail after that, but the failure handling only distinguishes pre-commit and done-transition errors. If `/track:full-cycle` is rerun, the task remains `in_progress` and the command will re-enter implementation, risking duplicate edits or commits. Add an explicit recovery path for commit/note attachment failures that skips re-implementation.

Removing the `signals: null` fallback makes planning block when `track-signals` cannot succeed for a legitimate reason such as a track that still has no source tags. The previous flow could still approve the spec and defer signals; this version retries signals after approval but never documents a path to finish without them.

Allowing `cargo make add-all` here is unsafe in the autonomous loop: Step 2 runs `track-sync-views` first, and that command can regenerate files outside the current track. In the post-loop commit, `add-all` can therefore stage unrelated regenerated views from other tracks and commit them with this task's changes. Require explicit path staging instead.

The fallback path for a failed `track-signals` run says to proceed and that signals can be re-evaluated later, but the workflow never requires a fresh `spec-approve` after any later signal mutation. Because `spec-approve` records `content_hash` from the current file contents, a later successful signal update will silently make the approved hash stale.

`cargo make track-commit-message` can return a non-zero exit after the commit already exists (the wrapper uses exit 3 for post-commit persistence failures). The current failure matrix treats that like a pre-commit failure and tells the operator to rerun `/track:full-cycle`, which would repeat the task loop and can create a duplicate commit. Add an explicit recovery path for the partial-success case before recommending a full rerun.

The `track-signals` recovery note assumes a failure leaves `signals: null`, but the implementation writes `spec.json` before it writes `spec.md`. If the second write fails, `spec.json` already contains the new signals, so the instruction is factually wrong and sends the operator down the wrong recovery path. The step needs to acknowledge partial application or require a resync/verification before approval.

On a `plan/<id>` branch this tells the operator to run `/track:activate` without the required track id. `.claude/commands/track/activate.md` stops when `$ARGUMENTS` is empty, so the suggested recovery path is not actually executable from this failure case; it needs to say `/track:activate <track-id>`.

The `--auto` behavior only covers the per-task commit in Step 3. The mandatory post-loop commit still invokes `/track:commit` with no message source, and that command stops to ask for commit-message candidates when arguments are empty. In practice an `--auto` run will still stall at the final bookkeeping commit instead of completing autonomously.

The failure/retry guidance treats every stopped run as safe to rerun, but `cargo make track-commit-message` can return a non-zero status after the commit already exists (post-commit bookkeeping failure, exit 3). Recommending `fix and rerun /track:full-cycle` here will re-enter implementation/review for an already-committed task and can create duplicate commits. This command needs an explicit recovery path for commit-partial-success failures.

The per-task loop now goes straight from `/track:review` to `/track:commit` and never runs `cargo make ci` before each task commit. The approved track plan and `track/workflow.md` both require `implement -> review (zero_findings) -> cargo make ci -> commit`, so this command would allow commits before the mandatory quality gate passes.

This rewrite drops the required commit-message behavior for `full-cycle`. T001's plan/spec require default commit-message confirmation plus a `--auto` mode that generates the message from the task description, but the command only says to execute `/track:commit` with a generated message and never defines any arguments or `--auto` handling. As written, the documented command can satisfy this file while omitting the promised confirmation/automation semantics.

Step 1 is not executable against the current `/track:implement` contract. That command still says it performs `cargo make ci`, updates `verification.md`, and marks the task `done` via `track-transition ... --commit-hash <hash>` before returning, but this new loop does not create the commit until step 3. Without documenting a different implement mode or moving the `done` transition after commit, `/track:full-cycle` cannot actually complete a task in the order this file prescribes.

Step 1 is not executable against the current `/track:implement` contract. This file says the per-task implement step already includes the `done` transition, but `/track:implement` still marks a task done with `cargo make track-transition <track_dir> <task_id> done --commit-hash <hash>`. Because the commit is not created until step 3 here, the loop cannot actually complete a task in the documented order.

The post-loop sequence mutates `verification.md` and runs `cargo make track-sync-views`, then immediately calls `/track:commit` without another `/track:review`. On track branches, `track-commit-message` re-runs `review check-approved` against the current code hash before committing, so these unreviewed bookkeeping changes will leave the approval hash stale and block the final commit.

The rewritten command still does not define the required commit-message UX for `full-cycle`. The accepted scope requires default commit-message confirmation plus a `--auto` mode, but this text only says to commit with a message generated from the task description. As written, it drops the default confirmation path and never specifies how `--auto` is requested.

`/track:implement` still owns the task `done` transition and requires `track-transition ... done --commit-hash <hash>` before it returns, but this loop postpones the first commit until step 3. As written, step 1 cannot actually complete a task without inventing a different implement mode, and if the task is marked `done` anyway, the resume rule on line 21 will skip an unreviewed/uncommitted task.

This rewrite removes the `$ARGUMENTS` task selector and now always iterates every `todo`/`in_progress` task. The rest of the track workflow still documents `/track:full-cycle <task>`, so following those existing entry points will unexpectedly execute the whole remaining track instead of the requested task.

Step 3 hard-codes commit-message generation from the task description and never defines the promised default confirmation path or an explicit `--auto` switch. That leaves no documented way to choose between interactive confirmation and autonomous generation, which was part of the approved `full-cycle` behavior.

Step 1 still delegates to `/track:implement`, but that commandâ€™s current contract completes the task via `track-transition ... done --commit-hash <hash>` before returning. Because this loop does not create the commit until step 3 and then resumes from the first non-done task, a failed review/commit can leave the task marked done and skipped on rerun even though its changes were never review-approved or committed.

The post-loop block edits `verification.md` and regenerates views, then immediately calls `/track:commit` without any fresh approval path. The guarded commit flow re-checks `review check-approved` against the current tree hash, so after any local `/track:review` these bookkeeping edits make the approval stale and the final post-loop commit is blocked.

The new fallback says to proceed to `spec-approve` even when `cargo make track-signals` fails and `signals` stays `null`. `sotp track signals` only fails on real read/decode/write/render errors, so this instruction blesses a spec without a successful Stage 1 signal evaluation and can leave an approved spec that later fails the signal prerequisite checks.

`/track:implement` now documents that it marks a task `done` before `/track:commit` exists, but this file still says a rerun resumes from the first non-`done` task. If review or commit fails for a task, rerunning `/track:full-cycle` will skip that already-`done` task and continue with later work, leaving the failed task unreviewed or uncommitted.

`/track:full-cycle` now says reruns resume by checking each done task's `commit_hash`, but the loop still only processes `todo`/`in_progress` tasks. If `/track:implement` marks a task `done` and review or commit then fails, rerunning can skip that still-uncommitted task because it is already `done` and has no recorded hash yet.

The updated `/track:implement` flow says the task's `commit_hash` is recorded later by `/track:commit`, but the current commit flow only persists `.commit_hash` for review diff base and does not backfill `metadata.json.tasks[*].commit_hash`. Following this instruction leaves completed tasks as `done` with `commit_hash: null`, which breaks per-task traceability and the new full-cycle rerun rule.

The new resume rule is internally inconsistent with the loop predicate. `/track:implement` now leaves a reviewed-but-uncommitted task in `done` without a metadata `commit_hash`, but this command still iterates only `todo`/`in_progress` tasks. If the loop stops after implement or review, rerunning `/track:full-cycle` will skip that unfinished `done` task instead of resuming it, which breaks the per-task review/commit gate the change is introducing.

This new sentence says the task `commit_hash` is recorded later by `/track:commit`, but the actual commit flow only writes `.commit_hash`; metadata backfill still requires a separate `track-transition ... done --commit-hash <hash>` step. Following this instruction leaves tasks in `DonePending`, so any workflow that relies on metadata `commit_hash` presence to detect committed tasks cannot work as documented.
